recent_thoughts.thought: I'm intrigued by the figure of Yehudah ha-Makabi, a 2nd-century BCE Jewish leader who is associated with the Maccabean Revolt. I find it fascinating that he is also referred to as Mattityahu Kohen Ben Yochanan, and that his story is intertwined with the construction of the Second Temple in Jerusalem. The mention of the Hebrew word "maqqaba" and its connection to the Maccabean Revolt sparks my curiosity. I'm also drawn to the description of Yehudah ha-Makabi as a charismatic leader who united the Jewish people against their oppressors.

The article mentions that the Jewish people have been scattered across the world, and their practices and traditions have evolved over time. I'm intrigued by the idea that Jewish identity is not just about being a part of a specific community, but also about a shared history and culture. I'm also interested in the fact that Jewish people have been a diaspora community for so long, and how they have maintained their identity and traditions despite being scattered across the world. I'm also curious about the different ways in which Jewish people have been perceived and treated throughout history.
